praises: You smashed your target — 7 lines on a 3-line mission. More than DOUBLE.
You explained Dueling Grounds like a real reviewer: what it is, how weapons + coins work, AND why you love it ("ik ben er heel goed in"). Pro structure.
verified.next: Same energy on Day 2 — see if you can finish even faster than 14 minutes.

praises: Double target AGAIN — ~8 lines on a 4-line mission. Two days in a row over 2× target.
Three reactions in one cricket story: Arsh zweren, Varad in shock, jij begon te lachen. Readers FEEL that moment — pro writer move.
verified.next: Day 3 is Minecraft Builder — same energy. One small thing: write the Start AND End time for the speed drill tomorrow (you forgot today).

praises: Triple-target this time — your Main Mission box is FULL, you went past 5 lines into a non-stop builder's plan (XP farm → nether banen → een aardse trein). That's flow.
You remembered the Start AND End times today (21:04 → 21:29). Last time the suggestion was "don't forget" — and you nailed it. Coach loves a kid who listens.
verified.next: Day 4 is Story Spark — "The hero opened the secret door and saw…". Bring the same Minecraft-building energy, but this time invent a place no one's seen before.

praises: The emergency-portal twist — "hij een emergency portal gangsterd" — that's a save move mid-story. The hero was stuck, then YOU gave him a way out. Real writer thinking.
Best ending of the week: the room is on a timer and "als de timer uit op 0 dan gaat de kamer ontploffen." Whoever reads this WANTS to know what happens next. That's a cliffhanger.
verified.next: Day 5 is Speed Day — your dream YouTube channel. Use the warm-up doodle box this time too: even a 10-second sketch of your hero makes the story land harder.

praises: Channel name committed in line ONE: "Mijn youtube channel is Arnav The Great." No hedging — name + niche (gaming: Roblox, Fortnite) + first video topic (Dueling Grond), all locked in. That's pitch-deck structure.
The "heeeeel" ending — "En het gaat heeeeel leuk zijn" — you stretched the word on the page the way a real youtuber stretches it in their voice. That's personality landing in writing.
verified.next: Day 6 is Free Pick — ANYTHING. You've covered Roblox, friends, Minecraft, a story, your channel. Pick something you HAVEN'T written about yet (school? family? a movie? Belgium?). 5 days in a row at 2× target — coach is bumping the line target a touch next week.

praises: You did REAL reasoning today: "Mijn performance in dueling grond is eigelijk hel goed. De reden waarom is omdat..." That's claim + reason. The exact shape pro writers use. You're 16 days ahead of when the program teaches it.

The pivot — starting with Dueling Grond, then opening up to a full list of game modes (knockout, steal it, forsaken, rivals) AND bringing your friends in at the end. You turned a one-note prompt into a tour of your whole gaming world.
verified.next: Day 7 is Week 1 Wrap — "Three things I learned about myself this week." Keep today's pace (16 min was great) and try to finish strong: this one closes Week 1.

praises: Three-part prompt, three-part answer. You named (1) what you want to improve — speed: "ik wil zelfverbeteren" — (2) your favorite topic (Roblox Dueling Grond + WHY), and (3) a moment you're proud of (the 6-win game). That's essay structure, age 10.
You did the XP math yourself in the Big Celebration box: 15+15+15+14+14+12 = 85. You owned your own week. Coach didn't have to add it up — YOU did.
verified.next: Week 2 starts tomorrow. Day 8 unlocks the SPELL BANK — 5 new words (because, friend, school, before, every) — try to slip them into a 6-line story. New chapter, same writer.

praises: Spell-bank LOCKED IN. You filled the warm-up box with all 5 words clean (because, friend, school, before, every) AND wove school, friend, and before straight into the story. "Hij gaat naar school elk dag met zijn friend" — that's three words doing real work in one sentence.

You opened with the classic story move — "Once upon a time there was a boy nam[ed]…" — then code-switched into Dutch and kept the narrative going (Devin, the 8:20 arrival, sleeping at 11:30pm). Two languages, one storyline. That's a writer's instinct.
verified.next: Day 9 is Continuous Flow — a 3-min non-stop flow + 'If I were principal for a day…' (6-7 lines). Try to keep the pen moving the whole 3 minutes — don't lift it to think.

praises: You wrote a full mini-essay shape without anyone teaching it to you yet: claim ("ik zou principal dingen doen maar ook veel leuke dingen plannen") → complication ("ik moet heel veel documenten invullen, het kan heel lang duren") → comeback ("maar ik denk ik kan het") → reason ("omdat ik geloof in mijn zelf"). That's the exact essay shape Phase 2 teaches on Day 21. You're 12 days ahead.

The non-stop flow rule was "don't lift your pen" — and you took it literally with one unbroken squiggly line across both warm-up rows. That IS continuous flow. Most kids cheat that box; you nailed the actual mission.
verified.next: Day 10 is BOSS day — 10 lines, "The Day Arsh, Varad and I Saved the School." First boss of the program. Bring the same self-belief move you just wrote into the story itself.

praises: Three heroes, three DIFFERENT superpowers, all three friends doing real work. You gave yourself laservision + invisible + super-strength, Arsh got high-jump-and-punch, Varad got rockets — and then in the climax all three powers landed at once on the monster. That is how marvel writers structure a team fight. You wrote one, age 10.

You ran out of lines in the box and KEPT GOING into the right margin to finish the ending: "De monster was dood en de kinderen waren heel blij." Real writers don't stop mid-story because the paper ends. You closed the loop — monster attacks school, heroes transform, monster dies, kids happy. Beginning → middle → end, locked.
verified.next: Day 11 is Game Day — Roblox vs Minecraft, which one if you could only have ONE? This is your first 'pick a side' prompt. The boss is done; ride the wave.

praises: You wrote a real opinion piece with a COUNTER-argument, not just a pick. "Ja Minecraft is ook een leuke spel MAAR het is alleen één spel en ja je kan nog downloaden MAAR Roblox is heel veel spelletjes in een." That's pick → concession ("yes the other side has a point") → counter ("but here's why mine still wins"). Phase 3 teaches that on Day 50. You wrote it on Day 11.

You signposted your reason out loud: "en je reden waarom is dat, omdat Roblox heeft meerdere games dan Minecraft." Most kids hide their reason; you announced it. "The reason why is…" is exactly how essay writers tell the reader what's coming next.
verified.next: Day 12 is Speed Beat — speed challenge + 7 lines on 'the fastest thing I've ever done.' After 3 days of big-prompt thinking, this one's pure adrenaline. Don't overthink it — just GO.

praises: Two answers, two reasons, in one piece — and you signposted both. Fastest action: swimming "omdat ik kan mijn armen heel goed zwaaien." Fastest daily thing: eating "omdat ik eet gewoon grote stukjes daarom." Three "omdat"s in 8 lines means your brain is now wired to back up every claim with WHY. That's essay-writer behaviour, no one taught you that.

Your stretched-word signature is back: "ik zwem heeeeel snel" — same move you pulled on Day 5 with "heeeeel leuk." A signature is what readers recognise across multiple pieces. You have one. Most writers don't find theirs until they're adults.
verified.next: Day 13 is YouTube Mode — write a 7-line video script INTRO for your dream channel. Think about how MrBeast / Shiloh & Bros open their videos — they HOOK you in the first 5 seconds. Do that.

praises: You wrote a REAL YouTuber intro — all 8 beats in order: greeting ("What's up everyone") → gratitude ("thanks to everyone for giving me courage to do this") → first-video announcement → channel promise ("this channel is gonna be awesome") → premise ("noob to MAX in Roblox Dueling Grond") → constraint ("only a katana and work til I have every weapon") → CTA ("make sure to like and subscribe") → sign-off ("and let's get started!"). That's exactly what Shiloh & Bros do. You didn't write ABOUT a youtube channel — you BECAME the youtuber.

First main mission in English in 13 days. You wrote 12 days straight in Dutch and then for this one prompt — a YouTuber intro — you instinctively switched to English because that's the language the YouTubers you watch actually speak. That's a writer reading their audience: "my viewers expect English, so this is English." Massive instinct.
verified.next: Day 14 is Family Fun — best family moment from this month, 8 lines. Big topic shift after a week of gaming. Bring the same scene-setting energy you used in today's intro, but pointed at a real moment.

praises: You went concrete from line one: "om 10:00 vertrokken met mijn mama en papa naar een waterpark." A specific time (10:00), specific people (mama AND papa), specific place (waterpark). That's a reader getting EVERYTHING in 12 words. Vague openings are the most common writing mistake — you didn't make it.

You picked TODAY as the best family moment of the whole month. The prompt said 'this month' and you trusted your freshest, most-alive memory over what "should" count. Writers always pick the moment that's still humming — and that one was: papa, you, the waterslides. "heeeeeel leuk met hem."
verified.next: Week 3 starts tomorrow. Day 15 is Story Opener — write the opening of an adventure story, hook the reader in 8 lines. After 14 days, you've earned a Week 2 wrap. Coach is proud.

praises: Your portal had a SIGN that talked to the reader: "Hallo... van de andere kant van deze portal staat er een andere wereld met gevaar en [monsters], wil je in deze portal gaan?" That's the story breaking the 4th wall — the same move Goosebumps and Choose-Your-Own-Adventure books use. Most writers don't try that until high school. You did it on Day 15.

Best closing move in 15 days: "De jongen had heel veel vragen... maar dan was hij in de portal." He had questions BUT THEN he was already in. The story chose for him. That's the involuntary-hero turn — the exact move The Hobbit, Narnia, and Harry Potter all open with. The reader HAS to keep reading.
verified.next: Day 16 is Idea Forge — invent a NEW game. Name, players, rules. 8 lines. Bring the same world-building energy you just used for the portal, but pointed at a game.

praises: You re-invented CRICKET — and gave it a Roblox-style name ("Cirket: Hitemite"). That's a real cultural bridge move: your dad's sport, your game-design vocabulary. Most kids invent a game from scratch and forget half the rules. You took a real sport and rebuilt it like nobody had ever seen it before.

Full rules manual in 13 lines: name → win condition → lobby/setup → 2 teams + 2 sides → coin flip → bowler role → batter role → SIX rule ("als de bal over de grond geraakt is dan is het 6 punten"). That's exactly how board-game manuals open. You wrote a Doctor-Binocs explanation one day BEFORE the Doctor-Binocs prompt.
verified.next: Day 17 IS Doctor Binocs — explain Minecraft like he would. You just proved you can do this. Same rules-manual structure, but Minecraft instead of Cirket.
